data.name: Coursework
data.title: Texas A&M Engineering Coursework (Python, C++, C, Java)
data.summary: Self-taught programming foundations built alongside a Civil Engineering course load: algorithm design, memory management, and object-oriented programming across Python, C++, C, and Java.
data.closingQuote: Every language is a set of trade-offs, and part of the job is knowing when you need low-level control and when you want high-level abstraction.
content: I started college at Texas A&M in Civil Engineering, and honestly, I picked it because I was lost. I knew I wanted engineering, since I liked building things and I was good at math, but civil was mostly the safe answer. The world will always need roads and bridges, so the jobs would always be there. It seemed like a plan.

The plan started coming apart in the programming courses, which were only there as requirements. The assignment I still remember is coding a chess game. It sounds simple until you're the one deciding everything: how to store the board, how each piece's movement gets encoded, how capturing works, how to keep a knight from jumping off the edge of the world. Every rule I'd taken for granted as a player had to be spelled out from nothing, and every one was its own little puzzle.

That was the first time schoolwork didn't feel like schoolwork. My engineering problems usually had one right answer waiting at the end of a formula, but here there were a dozen ways to represent one board, and the quality of the solution depended on how I thought about it. Write it, run it, watch it break, understand why, rewrite it. Before long I was spending more time on the programming assignments than on the classes they were attached to.

Along the way, that coursework quietly built me a real foundation: algorithm design, memory management, and object-oriented programming across Python, C++, C, and Java. Moving between four languages that quickly also taught me a lesson that never left: every language is a set of trade-offs, and part of the job is knowing when you need low-level control and when you want high-level abstraction.

Somewhere in that last year, the interest escaped the classroom. I started tinkering with a personal website, a side project I was thoroughly unqualified for at the time. Most of it confused me. I kept at it anyway. (That's a later chapter of this story, but you're looking at it right now.)

By the end of 2022, I stopped pretending the answer wasn't obvious. I left A&M and moved into Computer Science. Walking away from two years of an engineering degree, and from the safe path I'd chosen on purpose, is still the hardest decision I've made and the one I'm most glad about.

And that first chess assignment never really left either. Years later I built Jumpy, a game AI that packs an entire board into a single 16-bit integer. Same puzzle, better tools. You can play against it on this site.

data.name: Associate of Science
data.title: Collin Computer Science Coursework (Python, C++, Java)
data.summary: Associate of Science, Magna Cum Laude — Data Structures, Computer Architecture, and Discrete Mathematics, completed while transferring into a four-year CS program.
data.closingQuote: I'd learned to code by needing code. Collin was where I learned why the tools worked underneath.
content: Collin College was never supposed to be a chapter of its own. It started as pure practicality: a local college where prerequisites cost a fraction of university tuition, so I enrolled there the same semester I started at A&M and knocked out core classes on the side.

When I left A&M at the end of 2022, Collin changed jobs. It became the bridge. I had a target, UT Dallas for a CS degree, and Collin was where I stacked up the computer science prerequisites to get there: Introduction to Programming, Data Structures, Computer Architecture, Discrete Mathematics. Coming from A&M, I'd learned to code by needing code. Collin was where I learned why the tools worked underneath.

Even after transferring to UTD, I kept taking classes at Collin whenever a course could be done cheaper there, running both schools in parallel. I was optimizing for one thing: finishing a CS degree without drowning in tuition.

Then in 2025, an Associate of Science degree showed up in my mail. Magna cum laude. I hadn't applied for it, hadn't planned it, and didn't know I'd earned it. I'd apparently just taken enough of the right classes that Collin awarded it automatically. It's the only credential I've ever earned by accident, and honestly, it might be the most efficient thing I've ever done.

data.name: Bachelor of Science
data.title: UTD Computer Science Coursework (Python, C++, SQL, Bash)
data.summary: Bachelor of Science, Computer Science — coursework in Automata Theory, UNIX Programming, and Software Engineering, capped by a full-stack capstone with a six-person team.
data.closingQuote: Software Engineering taught me how code survives contact with other people's code.
content: UT Dallas was what the whole detour had been aiming at: finally studying computer science on purpose instead of around the edges of an engineering degree. I transferred in January 2023 with the fundamentals from Collin and two years of knowing exactly why I wanted to be there.

The coursework is where things got deep. Discrete Math for Computing and Automata Theory rebuilt how I think about computation itself, UNIX Programming made the command line feel like home (which explains a lot about how this site looks), and Software Engineering taught me how code survives contact with other people's code.

Some courses left artifacts you can still touch. The relational database running live on this site started as my UTD database coursework, adapted into a working demo. And my Artificial Intelligence course had an assignment that was pure 2020s: craft a single prompt that would get ChatGPT to produce the best algorithm for a board game. I did the assignment, but afterward I couldn't put the game down. It was simple enough to reason about and interesting enough to be worth solving, so I built it for real: the game itself, and the AI opponent to play it. That became Jumpy.

But the thing that actually decided my direction wasn't a course. It was this website. I'd started it back at A&M as a side project, and for a long time it was beyond me. Too complicated, too confusing, iteration after iteration borrowed from other portfolios I admired online. Then around mid-2023, something flipped. I'd gotten skilled enough to picture exactly what I wanted and then build it, and the moment those two connected, my interest exploded. Designing a site, it turns out, is the same act as making art: composition, color, rhythm, aesthetic. For the first time, one project could hold everything I loved in a single place.

That's when frontend stopped being one option among many and became the obvious one. The TUI aesthetic you're looking at is where all those iterations landed. It all funneled into my capstone, The Review Mediator, a semester building a full-stack platform with a team of six — that one gets its own entry. I graduated in December 2025.

data.name: Web Development
data.title: Full-Stack Web Dev (React, Express, Node.js, Supabase)
data.summary: UTD capstone, team of six — role-based dashboards with Clerk auth, JWT-secured Express middleware, and Supabase row-level security policies for a full-stack review platform.
data.closingQuote: I did my rotations through backend and database work, and when they ended, I went back to frontend by choice.
content: Everything before this entry, I built alone. The Review Mediator was the semester I found out what changes when five other developers are in the same codebase. It's a full-stack review management platform that lets businesses track and dispute their Google and Yelp reviews from one dashboard, built as my UTD capstone with a team of six.

The project was structured in one-week splits, with the team divided across frontend, backend, and database, and everyone rotating so nobody graduated knowing only their corner. I did my rotations through backend and database work, and when they ended, I went back to frontend by choice. By then that shouldn't surprise you. I spent the final stretch working directly with the platform's owner, turning what they wanted their pages to do into features that actually did it.

The work I'm proudest of sits underneath the UI, though. I implemented role-based dashboards with Clerk, with dynamic routing that sends each user where their role allows and nowhere else. That led me into the security layer, which became my favorite problem of the project. Review data is sensitive, so we protected it twice: I validated JSON Web Tokens in Express middleware so every request proved who it came from, and configured Supabase row-level security policies so that even if a request slipped past the first layer, the database itself would refuse to hand over another business's data. Two locks, two layers, no single point of failure.

As for the team, I'd been braced for drama that never came. Credit the rotation structure, a good senior developer, or six introverts who'd rather solve the problem than win the argument. The disagreements we had were about how to solve things, never about each other, and the real coordination skill turned out to be quieter: keeping the project moving as coursework pulled people away at different times, picking up threads someone else had to set down.
The Review Mediator is the most complete thing I've built so far. Real product, real team, and the first time everything before this entry got tested outside a classroom.

data.name: Work Experience
data.title: Error 404, Work Experience not found (NULL)
content: There appears to be a 404 error — no entries found in the Work Experience directory.
It's not a bug, it's just a chapter that hasn't been written yet.
Future employers, feel free to help populate this section! My resume is here, and everything above it is the story so far.
